text: Alright, let's do another installment of robotic cases. Today we're going to feature a robotic right inguinal hernia repair with mesh. It was an indirect hernia, I think, pretty straightforward case, but it does establish some of the important things that we look for in these cases.
This is a case recorded and then the videos at three x I remove the instrument exchanges.

The surgeon begins creating a peritoneal flap, cutting from right to left with an instrument in the right hand. The closure proceeds from medial to lateral, with the medial ligament being taken using energy to prevent potential recanalization.
The surgeon begins the procedure by orienting towards the medial landmarks, visually assessing the general location of the pubis and Cooper's ligament without specifically identifying them.
The surgeon begins dissection laterally, staying close to the peritoneum. The current focus is on the pre-transversalis plane, with plans to transition into the preparatory plane.

The surgeon initially works off plane to gain extra tissue for manipulation, then proceeds with the reduction of an indirect hernia, which descends easily. Following this, the surgeon begins developing the space for mesh placement.
The surgeon dissects in multiple layers, reflecting the testicular vasculature laterally while continuing to develop a space and pocket for mesh placement.

The surgeon resumes medial dissection, aiming to identify Cooper's ligament and the pubis. The focus is on an indirect defect, with plans to expose two centimeters of the pubis on the contralateral side.

The surgeon dissects the areolar tissues around Cooper's ligament, ensuring there is no femoral defect. The camera orientation is adjusted to focus on parietalizing the structures of the spermatic cord, allowing the mesh to lay flat. The thin peritoneum is visible as the surgeon lateralizes the vessels, carefully avoiding creating any holes.

The surgeon is working near the vasculature, specifically the external iliac vessels. The medial ligament is visible and has been disconnected from the vas deferens. The surgeon clarifies that the structure is the medial ligament, not the ureter.
The surgeon continues to develop the lateral pocket by parietalizing the vas deferens, ensuring no additional hernias are present, in preparation for mesh placement.

The surgeon carefully navigates the triangle of doom, avoiding the use of energy or excessive sharp dissection between the structures near the vein. Lymphatic tissue around the obturator is being removed to ensure the mesh sits properly. There is no peritoneal defect or true obturator hernia present, and the surgical field is well-exposed.

The surgeon exposes the midline and pubis, identifying Cooper's ligament and ensuring at least two centimeters of contralateral coverage. The surgical field is explored for direct, indirect, femoral, and obturator spaces, as well as a cord lipoma. An appropriately sized pocket is created for a large mesh, suitable for the patient's stature.

The surgeon ensures the mid-weight mesh is properly positioned, with the blue line seated over Cooper's ligament and the horizontal line placed over the iliac vessels, which are visibly pulsating behind the mesh.

The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap, starting from the lateral side and moving medially. The insufflation is reduced to about eight to facilitate closure, ensuring the lateral aspect is not overly tight and avoiding excessive integration of the medial peritoneum.

The surgeon approaches the epigastric vessels, carefully tightening the suture line to ensure there are no gaps. The suture line is completed and then reversed for three to four bites to secure the closure.
